مطلب.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مَطْلَب (matlab), borrowed from Arabic مَطْلَب (maṭlab), from طَلَبَ (ṭalaba). First attested in c. 1635 as Middle Hindi مطلب (mt̤lb /⁠matlab⁠/).

senses.g: one who is driven by selfish motives; selfish; opportunistic

ex.u: مطلق العنان بادشاہ
ex.e: absolute monarch

مطلق العنان.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مطلق العنان (mutlaq-ul'inān, “absolute, authoritarian”), from Arabic مُطْلَق (muṭlaq) (“absolute, unrestrained”) + Arabic العِنَان (al-ʕinān) (“control, rein”).

مطلقا.e: Borrowed from Arabic مُطْلَقًا (muṭlaqan), adverbial accusative of مُطْلَق (muṭlaq).

ex.u: وہ اپنے طریقوں کی اعلیٰ کارکردگی سے پوری طرح مطمئن ہوتا ہے۔
ex.r: vo apne tarīqõ kī a'lā kārkardagī se pūrī tarh mutmain hotā hai.
ex.e: He becomes completely satisfied with the excellent results of his methods.

ex.u: صِرف ناتجربہ کاروں کو دھوکا دے سکتا ہؠ اور مطمئن کر سکتا ہؠ؂۱۔
ex.r: sirf nātajurba kārõ ko dhokā de saktā hai aur mutmain kar saktā hai.
ex.e: It can only deceive and assure the inexperienced.[¹]

مطمئن.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مُطْمَئِن (mutma'in), from Arabic مُطْمَئِنّ (muṭmaʔinn).

ex.u: بدعنوانی کے خلاف مظاہرہ ہوا
ex.e: There was a demonstration against corruption.

senses.g: Muzaffarabad (a city, the capital of the autonomous territory of Azad Kashmir, Pakistan)
Muzaffarabad (a district of Azad Kashmir; in full, Muzaffarabad District)
Muzaffarabad (a division of Azad Kashmir; in full, Muzaffarabad Division)

مظفر آباد.e: From مُظَفَّر (muz̤affar) + آباد (-ābād), named after Sultan Muzaffar Khan who founded the city.

senses.g: Muzaffargarh (a city in Muzaffargarh district, Punjab, Pakistan)

معاش.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مَعَاش (ma'āš), borrowed from Arabic مَعَاش (maʕāš), from عَاشَ (ʕāša).

معاشرہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian معاشره (mu'āšara), from Arabic مُعَاشَرَة (social intercourse, companionship).

معاصر.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مُعَاصِر (mu'āsir), borrowed from Arabic مُعَاصِر (muʕāṣir), from عَاصَرَ (ʕāṣara).

معاف.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مُعَاف (mu'āf), borrowed from Arabic مُعَافًى (muʕāfan), from عَافَى (ʕāfā).

ex.u: حکومت نے اس معاملے کو نظر انداز کیا ہے
ex.r: ḥukūmat ne is mu'āmle ko naẓar andāz kiyā hai
ex.e: The government has ignored this matter.

معاملہ.e: borrowed from Classical Persian مُعَامَلَه (mu'āmala), from Arabic مُعَامَلَة (muʕāmala), from the root ع م ل (ʕ m l) related to work and deeds.

senses.g: contract; agreement; the matter which is legally enforceable

معاہدہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مُعَاهَدَه (mu'āhada), borrowed from Arabic مُعَاهَدَة (muʕāhada), from عَاهَدَ (ʕāhada).

معائنہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مُعَایِنَه (mu'āyina), borrowed from Arabic مُعَايَنَة (muʕāyana), from عَايَنَ (ʕāyana).

senses.g: Borrowed from Classical Persian مَعْبَد (ma'bad), from Arabic مَعْبَد (maʕbad).

ex.u: بھگوان ہوتا تو اس معبد کے اندر ہوتا، دہلیز پر نہ بیٹھا کرتا۔
ex.r: aśfāq ahmad
ex.e: If there was a god, he would have been inside this temple, not lingering at the entrance.

معبود.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian معبود (ma'būd), from Arabic مَعْبُود (maʕbūd).
Borrowed from Classical Persian معبود (ma'būd), from Arabic مَعْبُود (maʕbūd).

معتبر.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian معتبر (mu'tabar), from Arabic مُعْتَبَر (muʕtabar).

معجزہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مُعْجِزَه (mu'jiza), borrowed from Arabic مُعْجِزَة (muʕjiza). First attested in c. 1697 as Middle Hindi معجزہ (m'jzh).

معجم.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مُعْجَم (mu'jam), from Arabic مُعْجَم (muʕjam).

senses.g: an Arabic word used by non-Arabs
a Perso-Arabic word (ie. an Arabic word that is either influenced or borrowed through Persian)

معجم.e: From Arabic ع ج م (ʕ j m).

معدن.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مَعْدَن (ma'dan), borrowed from Arabic مَعْدِن (maʕdin), from عَدَنَ (ʕadana).

ex.u: میرے معدے میں درد ہو رہا ہے۔
ex.r: mere mi'de mẽ dard ho rahā hai.
ex.e: My stomach hurts.

معدہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian معده (mi'da), from Arabic مِعْدة (miʕda).

senses.g: pardon (ie. a rejection of an invite or offering)

معذرت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مَعْذِرَت (ma'zirat), borrowed from Arabic مَعْذِرَة (maʕḏira), from عَذَر (ʕaḏar). First attested in c. 1609 as Middle Hindi معذرت (m'ẕrt /⁠ma'ẕirat⁠/).
Borrowed from Classical Persian مَعْذِرَت (ma'zirat), borrowed from Arabic مَعْذِرَة (maʕḏira), from عَذَر (ʕaḏar). First attested in c. 1609 as Middle Hindi معذرت (m'ẕrt /⁠ma'ẕirat⁠/).

معذور.e: First attested in c. 1582 as Middle Hindi معذور (maʻẕūr), from Classical Persian معذور (maʻẕūr), itself from Arabic مَعْذُور (maʕḏūr).

معرکہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian معرکه (ma'raka), from Arabic مَعْرَكَة (maʕraka).

senses.g: a stretched or close vowel; an epithet for the و (vao) and ی (ye) letters

معروف.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مَعْرُوف (ma'rūf), borrowed from Arabic مَعْرُوف (maʕrūf), from عَرَفَ (ʕarafa).

معصوم.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مَعْصُوم (ma'sūm), borrowed from Arabic مَعْصُوم (maʕṣūm), from عَصِمَ (ʕaṣima). First attested in c. 1649 as Middle Hindi معصوم (m'ṣvm /⁠ma'sūm⁠/).
Borrowed from Classical Persian مَعْصُوم (ma'sūm), borrowed from Arabic مَعْصُوم (maʕṣūm), from عَصِمَ (ʕaṣima). First attested in c. 1649 as Middle Hindi معصوم (m'ṣvm /⁠ma'sūm⁠/).

معطل.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian معطل (mu'attal), from Arabic مُعَطَّل (muʕaṭṭal).

senses.g: the superior, the greater, the principal, chief; the greater part; the better sort; the gross, or mass, or bulk (of)

معلم.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مُعَلِّم (mu'allim), borrowed from Arabic مُعَلِّم (muʕallim), from عَلَّمَ (ʕallama).
Borrowed from Classical Persian مُعَلَّم (mu'allam), borrowed from Arabic مُعَلَّم (muʕallam), from عَلَّمَ (ʕallama).

senses.g: first teacher, first instructor, first master

معلمہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian معلمه (mu'allima, “female teacher”), from Arabic مُعَلِّمَة (muʕallima).

ex.u: معلوم نہیں
ex.r: mālūm nahī̃
ex.e: (I) don't know

معلوم.e: From Arabic مَعْلُوم (maʕlūm) (through Classical Persian معلوم (ma'lūm)), from عَلِمَ (ʕalima).

senses.g: the occupation of a builder, architect, mason

معماری.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مِعْمَارِی (mi'mārī), from مِعْمَار (mi'mār, “architect”) + ـِی (-ī, suffix forming abstract nouns). By surface analysis, مِعْمار (mi'mār) + ـِی (-ī).

معمول.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مَعْمُول (ma'mūl), borrowed from Arabic مَعْمُول (maʕmūl), from عَمِلَ (ʕamila).
Borrowed from Classical Persian مَعْمُول (ma'mūl), borrowed from Arabic مَعْمُول (maʕmūl), from عَمِلَ (ʕamila).

معمولی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مَعْمُولِی (ma'mūlī), from معمول + ـی (-ē). By surface analysis, مَعْمُول (ma'mūl, “usual”) + ی (ī).

معنوی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مَعْنَوِی (ma'nawī), from Arabic مَعْنَوِيّ (maʕnawiyy).

معنی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مَعْنِی (ma'nī)/مَعْنَیٰ (ma'nā), from Arabic مَعْنَى (maʕnā).

senses.g: example; a good attribute (ie. of a person)

معیار.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مِعْیَار (mi'yār), from Arabic مِعْيَار (miʕyār), with the ma'rūf vowel shifted to a majhūl vowel (i > e).

معیشت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian معیشت (ma'īšat), from Arabic مَعِيشَة (maʕīša).

senses.g: West Asia (the westernmost geographic region of Asia, which includes Anatolia, the Arabian Peninsula, Iran, the Levant, Mesopotamia, the Sinai Peninsula, and Transcaucasia)
West Bengal (a state in eastern India)
West Pakistan (a former province of Pakistan, now the entirety of the country of Pakistan)

مغل.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مُغُل (muġul), ultimately from Middle Mongol ᠮᠣᠩᠬᠣᠯ (mongqol).

ex.u: ہم جنگ کے بجاے، مفاہمت سے اپنے مسائل سلجھا سکتے ہیں۔
ex.r: ham jaṅg ke ba-jāe, mufāhimat se apne masāil suljhā sakte ha͠i.
ex.e: We can solve our problems with understanding instead of war.

ex.u: ”مفاہمت کی سیاست“ ہر سیاستداں کے پسندیدہ الفاظ ہیں۔
ex.r: “mufāhimat kī siyāsat” har siyāsatdān ke pasandīdah alfāz ha͠i.
ex.e: “The politics of compromise” is the favourite phrase of every politician.

ex.u: کل دُنیاوی ترقیوں کے دروازے ہمارے لئے مفتوح تھے۔
ex.r: kal dunyāvī taraqqiyõ ke darvāze hamāre liye maftūh the.
ex.e: Yesterday, the gates to the materialistic advancements were open for us.

مفتوح.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مفتوح (maftūḥ), from Arabic مَفْتُوح (maftūḥ).

مفتی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مُفْتی (muftī), from Arabic مُفْتٍ (muftin).

مفہوم.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مفهوم (mafhūm), from Arabic مَفْهُوم (mafhūm).

مقاومت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مُقاوَمَت (muqāwamat), from Arabic مُقَاوَمَة (muqāwama).

ex.u: یہ علاقہ مقبوض ہے۔
ex.r: ye ilāqā maqbūz hai.
ex.e: This area is occupied.

مقبوض.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مقبوض (maqbūz), from Arabic مَقْبُوض (maqbūḍ).

ex.u: اسرائیل کو مقبوضہ فلسطین سمجھا جاتا ہے۔
ex.r: isrāīl ko maqbūza falastīn samjhā jātā hai.
ex.e: Israel is often regarded as occupied Palestine.

senses.g: the de facto India union territories of Jammu and Kashmir and Ladakh collectively called "Indian-Administered Kashmir", one of three parts that together form the dejure province of Kashmir, the other being Azad Kashmir and Aksai Chin.
Jammu and Kashmir (a union territory in northern India; capital: Jammu and Srinagar)

مقبول.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مقبول (maqbūl), from Arabic مَقْبُول (maqbūl), passive participle of قَبِلَ (qabila, “to accept”).

مقبولیت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مَقْبُولِیَّت (maqbūliyyat), from Arabic مَقْبُولِيَّة (maqbūliyya). Equivalent to مَقْبُول (maqbūl) + ـیَت (-iyat).

senses.g: the part of the body which causes death, if hurt or damaged

مقتل.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مَقْتَل (maqtal), itself from Arabic مَقْتَل (maqtal). First attested as Middle Hindi مَقْتَل (maqtal) in 1503 A.D.

senses.g: saint (chiefly used by Urdu & Punjabi speaking Christians for their saints)

ex.u: مقدس یوحنا رسول۔
ex.r: muqaddas yuhanna rasul.
ex.e: Saint John the Apostle.

senses.g: a female saint (chiefly used by Urdu-speaking Christians for their female saints)

ex.u: مُقَدَّسَہ مَرْیَم، یَسُوع کِی ماں۔
ex.r: muqaddasa maryam, yasū' kī mā̃.
ex.e: Saint Mary, mother of Jesus.

مقدسہ.e: Borrowed from Arabic مُقَدَّسَة (muqaddasa), feminine of مُقَدَّس (muqaddas).

مقدمہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مقدمه (muqaddama), from a variant of Arabic مُقَدِّمة (muqaddima).

senses.g: Macedonia (a geographic region in Southeast Europe in the Balkans, including North Macedonia and parts of Greece, Bulgaria, Albania and Serbia)
Macedonia (an ancient Greek kingdom in Southeast Europe)
Macedonia (former name of North Macedonia: a country in Southeast Europe in the Balkans)
Macedonia (a geographic region and former administrative region of Greece)
Macedonia (a geographic region in southwestern Bulgaria; in full, Pirin Macedonia or Bulgarian Macedonia)
Macedonia (a former constituent republic of Yugoslavia)
Macedonia (a former province of the Roman Empire)

مقروض.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مَقْرُوض (maqrūz), from Arabic مَقْرُوض (maqrūḍ).
Borrowed from Classical Persian مَقْرُوض (maqrūz), from Arabic مَقْرُوض (maqrūḍ).

مقصد.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مَقْصَد (maqsad), borrowed from Arabic مَقْصَد (maqṣad).

مقصود.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مَقْصُود (maqsūd), from Arabic مَقْصُود (maqṣūd).
Borrowed from Classical Persian مَقْصُود (maqsūd), from Arabic مَقْصُود (maqṣūd).

senses.g: the last line of a qasida or ghazal

مقناطیس.e: From Arabic مَغْنَاطِيس (maḡnāṭīs), ultimately of Ancient Greek origin. Akin to Pashto مقناطيس.

مقناطيس.e: From Arabic مَغْنَاطِيس (maḡnāṭīs), ultimately of Ancient Greek origin. Akin to Pashto مقناطيس.

مقننہ.e: Borrowed from Arabic مُقَنِّنَة (muqannina), feminine of مُقَنِّن (muqannin).

مقید.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مُقَیَّد (muqayyad), borrowed from Arabic مُقَيِّد (muqayyid), from قَيَّدَ (qayyada).

مقیم.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مُقِیم (muqīm), from Arabic مُقِيم (muqīm).

مکا.e: Inherited from Prakrit *𑀫𑀓𑁆𑀓𑀕 (*makkaga), from Sanskrit मर्कक (markaka, “Ardea argala”).

مکالمہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مُکَالَمَه (mukālama), from Arabic مُكَالَمَة (mukālama).

مکان.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مَکَان (makān), borrowed from Arabic مَكَان (makān).

مکتب.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مَکْتَب (maktab), froml Arabic مَكْتَب (maktab).

مکروہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مکروه (makrūh), from Arabic مَكْرُوه (makrūh).

مکڑی.e: Derived from Sanskrit मर्कट (markaṭa) + Middle Indo-Aryan -𑀇𑀓𑀸- (-ikā-).

ex.u: اَوَّل مَکْسُور
ex.r: avval maksūr
ex.e: first letter [is followed by a] kasra

مکسور.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مکسور (maksūr), from Arabic مَكْسُور (maksūr).

senses.g: complete (ie. in its entirety; accomplished, finalised; perfected)
finished (ie. to come to an end; concluded)

مکمل.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مُکَمَّل (mukammal), from Arabic مُكَمَّل (mukammal). First attested in c. 1765 as Middle Hindi مکمل (mkml /⁠mukammal⁠/).

مکنون.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مَکْنُون (maknūn), from Arabic مَكْنُون (maknūn).

senses.g: Mecca (a large city, the provincial capital of Mecca Province in the in the Hejaz region, Saudi Arabia, the holiest place in Islam, location of the sacred Ka'ba, and to which Muslims are required to make a hajj at least once in their lifetime, if possible)

مکہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مَکَّه (makka), from Arabic مَكَّة (makka). First attested in c. 1564 as Middle Hindi مکہ (mkh /⁠makka⁠/).

مکھ.e: Borrowed from Sanskrit मुख (mukha). Doublet of مُن٘ہ (mũh).

senses.g: making any continuous or tedious sound
the opening melodic phase of a piece of music.

مکھن.e: Inherited from Prakrit मक्खण (makkhaṇa), from Sanskrit म्रक्षण (mrakṣaṇa).

مکھی.e: Inherited from Prakrit 𑀫𑀓𑁆𑀔𑀺𑀆 (makkhiā), from Sanskrit मक्षिका (makṣikā). Cognate with Punjabi ਮੱਖੀ (makkhī).

مگر.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مگر (magar, “but, unless, perhaps”), from Middle Persian 𐭠𐭫 𐭧𐭲 (ʾl ḥt /⁠ma agar⁠/, “perhaps; lest (lit. not if)”).
Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀫𑀕𑀭 (magara), from Sanskrit मकर (mákara).

ملاحظہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian ملاحظه (mulāhaza), from Arabic مُلَاحَظَة (mulāḥaẓa).

senses.g: waiting on, assiduity, diligence, close attendance

ملازمت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مُلَازَمَت (mulāzamat), from Arabic مُلَازَمَة (mulāzama).

ملاقات.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مُلَاقَات (mulāqāt), from Arabic مُلَاقَاة (mulāqāh).

ملامت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مَلَامَت (malāmat), from Arabic مَلَامَة (malāma).

senses.g: to cause to meet; to match

ملائم.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مُلَایِم (mulāyim), from Arabic مُلَائِم (mulāʔim).

ملبوس.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مَلْبُوس (malbūs), from Arabic مَلْبُوس (malbūs).
Borrowed from Classical Persian مَلْبُوس (malbūs), from Arabic مَلْبُوس (malbūs).

senses.g: Multan (a city in Multan district, Punjab, Pakistan)

ملتان.e: From Saraiki ملتان (multān), perhaps from Sanskrit मुलस्थान (mulasthāna), from मुल (mula, “an ancient Hindu tribe”) + स्थान (sthāna).

ملزم.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian ملزم (mulzim), from Arabic مُلْزِم (mulzim).
Borrowed from Classical Persian ملزم (mulzim), from Arabic مُلْزِم (mulzim).

ex.u: نِیُوزی لَین٘ڈ اَور بَرْطانیَہ بَہُت خوش حال مَمالِک ہَیں۔
ex.r: niyūzī-laiṇḍ aur bartānya bahot xuś-hāl mamālik ha͠i.
ex.e: New Zealand and the UK are very prosperous countries.

ex.u: مُلْکِ فَنا
ex.r: mulk-i fanā
ex.e: mortal realm

ملک.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مُلْک (mulk), from Arabic مُلْك (mulk). First attested as Middle Hindi ملک (mulk).

ex.u: میرِی خوابوں کی مَلِکَہ
ex.r: merī xvābõ kī malika
ex.e: queen of my dreams

ملکہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian ملکه (malika), from Arabic مَلِكَة (malika), feminine of مَلِك (malik, “king”).

senses.g: belonging to one's own country; indigenous, national, local, domestic
pertaining to the country or government; civil; political

ملکی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian ملکی (mulkī), from Arabic مُلْكِيّ (mulkiyy). Equivalent to مُلْک (mulk, “country”) + ـی (-ī).

ملکیت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مِلِکِیَّت (milikiyyat), from Arabic مِلْكِيَّة (milkiyya).

senses.g: dirty, filthy, foul, impure, soiled, unclean
tarnished, stained, sullied, tainted, rusty, polluted, depraved, sinful, bad, vile, vicious

ملنا.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀫𑀺𑀮𑀤𑀺 (miladi), from Sanskrit मिलति (milati).

ملنگ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian ملنگ (malang, “jovial, lively, ecstasied, tipsy”).

ملی.e: Inherited from Prakrit 𑀫𑀮𑁆𑀮𑀺𑀆 (malliā), from Sanskrit मल्लिका (mallikā).

ex.u: باغِ مِلّی
ex.r: bāġ-i millī
ex.e: national park

ملی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian ملی (millī), from Arabic مِلِّيّ (milliyy).

ex.u: یہ خلقت قدرت میں نمودار ہے اور توحید میں ملیامیٹ۔
ex.r: maulānā śāh gul ḥussan
ex.e: In natural creation it is apparent and [while] in monotheism it is lost.

ملیامیٹ.e: First attested in c. 1534 as Middle Hindi ملیامیٹ (mlyamyṭ /⁠mlyāmyṭ⁠/), from ملی (milī, “found”) + ا (ā) + میٹ، میٹا/مٹانا (meṭ, meṭā, miṭānā, “erased”). Unrelated to English eliminate or annihilate. Compare Punjabi مِلِیامیٹ (milīyāmeṭ).

senses.g: a term part of an ancient game;

ملیامیٹ.e: First attested in c. 1534 as Middle Hindi ملیامیٹ (mlyamyṭ /⁠mlyāmyṭ⁠/), from ملی (milī, “found”) + ا (ā) + میٹ، میٹا/مٹانا (meṭ, meṭā, miṭānā, “erased”). Unrelated to English eliminate or annihilate. Compare Punjabi مِلِیامیٹ (milīyāmeṭ).

ممالک.e: Borrowed from Arabic مَمالِك (mamālik), plural of مَمْلَكَة (mamlaka).

ممانی.e: From مامُوں (māmū̃, “maternal uncle; mother's brother”) + ـَانی (-ānī).

senses.g: Mombasa (a large coastal city in Kenya)
Mumbai (a megacity, the capital of Maharashtra, India, also known as Bombay)

ممتاز.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مُمْتَاز (mumtāz), from Arabic مُمْتَاز (mumtāz).

senses.g: a unisex given name, Mumtaz, from Arabic

ممتاز.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مُمْتَاز (mumtāz), from Arabic مُمْتَاز (mumtāz).

ممکن.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian ممکن (mumkin), from Arabic مُمْكِن (mumkin).

مملکت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مَمْلَکَت (mamlakat), from Arabic مَمْلَكَة (mamlaka). First attested in c. 1635 as Middle Hindi مملکت (mmlkt /⁠mamlakat⁠/).

senses.g: United Kingdom (a kingdom and country in Northern Europe)

مملکت متحدہ.e: Borrowed from Arabic الْمَمْلَكَة الْمُتَّحِدَة (al-mamlaka l-muttaḥida).

ممنوع.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مَمْنُوع (mamnū'), borrowed from Arabic مَمْنُوع (mamnūʕ), from مَنَعَ (manaʕa).

ممی.e: Borrowed from English mummy. Doublet of مومِیا (momiyā).

من.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian من (man), ultimately from Akkadian 𒈠𒈾 (manûm).

senses.g: darling, dear, boy (especially of a child)

منا.e: Inherited from Prakrit 𑀫𑀼𑀁𑀟 (muṃḍa) + Middle Indo-Aryan -𑀓- (-ka-), from Sanskrit मुण्ड (muṇḍa). Cognate with Punjabi ਮੁੰਡਾ (muṇḍā), ਮੁੰਨਾ (munnā).

منات.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مَنَات (manāt), from Arabic مَنَاة (manāh).

ex.u: خوشگوار و مُناسِب ہے وطن خاطر مرنا۔
ex.r: xuśgwār ō munāsib hai watan xātir marna.
ex.e: It is pleasant and appropriate to die for one's homeland.

مناسبت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مُنَاسَبَت (munāsabat), from Arabic مُنَاسَبَة (munāsaba).

مناظر.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مَنَاظِر (manāzir), from Arabic مَنَاظِر (manāẓir).

مناعت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مناعت (manā'at), from Arabic مَناعَة (manāʕa).

منافقانہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian منافقانه۔ By surface analysis, انہ + منافق

منانا.e: From Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀫𑀡𑁆𑀡𑀇 (maṇṇaï), from Sanskrit मन्यते (manyate).

senses.g: water channel laid from canal to field

منتخب.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian منتخب (muntaxab), taken from Arabic مُنْتَخَب (muntaḵab).

منتظم.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian منتظم (muntazim), from Arabic مُنْتَظِم (muntaẓim), active participle of اِنْتَظَمَ (intaẓama).

ex.u: میں دو منٹ میں آ رہا ہوں۔
ex.r: ma͠i do minaṭ mẽ ā rahā hū̃.
ex.e: I'm coming in two minutes.

منجمد.e: Borrowed from Arabic مُنْجَمِد (munjamid), from Arabic جُمُود (jumūd).

منحصر.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مُنْحَصِر (munhasir), from Arabic مُنْحَصِر (munḥaṣir).

منحوس.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مَنْحُوس (manhūs), from Arabic مَنْحُوس (manḥūs).

senses.g: temple, place or house of worship
without top leaves or branches (as a tree)
open at the toe (as a shoe)

منڈا.e: Inherited from Prakrit 𑀫𑀼𑀁𑀟 (muṃḍa) + Middle Indo-Aryan -𑀓- (-ka-), from Sanskrit मुण्ड (muṇḍa).

senses.g: a man or boy having a shaved head

منڈا.e: Inherited from Prakrit 𑀫𑀼𑀁𑀟 (muṃḍa) + Middle Indo-Aryan -𑀓- (-ka-), from Sanskrit मुण्ड (muṇḍa).

senses.g: Mandi Bahauddin (a city in Mandi Bahauddin district, Punjab, Pakistan)

منڈی بہاؤالدین.e: From Urdu منڈی (“market”), and named after Bahauddin, a Sufi saint.

منزل.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مَنزِل (manzil), from Arabic مَنْزِل (manzil, “house, dwelling”). First attested in c. 1421 as Old Hindi مَنْزِل (mnzl /⁠manzil⁠/, “abode”).

senses.g: cancelled, abolished, annulled, repealed, abrogated, erased

منسوخ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian منسوخ (mansūx), from Arabic مَنْسُوخ (mansūḵ).

منشور.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مَنْشُور (manšūr), from Arabic مَنْشُور (manšūr). First attested in c. 1684 as Middle Hindi منشور (mnśvr⁩ /⁠manśūr⁠/).
Borrowed from Classical Persian مَنْشُور (manšūr), from Arabic مَنْشُور (manšūr). First attested in c. 1684 as Middle Hindi منشور (mnśvr⁩ /⁠manśūr⁠/).

منشی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian منشی (munšī), from Arabic مُنْشِئ (munšiʔ). Cognate with Bengali মুনশি (munśi).
Ultimately from Arabic مُنَشِّئ (munaššiʔ) from the root ن ش ء (n š ʔ).

منشیہ.e: Learned borrowing from Sanskrit मनुष्य (manuṣyà). Doublet of مَنُکھ (manukh).

منصوبہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian منصوبه (mansūba), from Arabic مَنْصُوبَة (manṣūba).

منظر.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مَنْظَر (manzar), from Arabic مَنْظَر (manẓar).

منظم.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مُنَظَّم (munazzam), from Arabic مُنَظَّم (munaẓẓam).

منظور.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian منظور (manzūr), from Arabic مَنْظُور (manẓūr).
Borrowed from Classical Persian منظور (manzūr), from Arabic مَنْظُور (manẓūr).

منع.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مَنْع (man'), borrowed from Arabic مَنْع (manʕ), from مَنَعَ (manaʕa). First attested in c. 1591 as Middle Hindi منع (mn').

senses.g: unique, singular, apart, alone, separate, isolated

منفرد.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مُنفَرِد, from Arabic مُنْفَرِد (munfarid).

منقطع.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مُنْقَطِع (munqati'), from Arabic مُنْقَطِع (munqaṭiʕ).

منگل.e: Shortening of منگلوار (mangalvār), from Sanskrit मङ्गलवार (maṅgalavāra).

senses.g: to order (e.g. food, a drink, goods)

ex.u: باہر سے کچھ منگوانا ہے؟
ex.r: bāhir se kuch maṅgvānā hai?
ex.e: Do you want to order some takeaway?

senses.g: to cause to be asked for, requested

منگوانا.e: Urdu verb set مان٘گْنا (māṅgnā) مَن٘گْوَانا (maṅgvānā) Causative of مانگنا (māṅgnā).

ex.u: تُمْہارے مُن٘ہ میں کْیا ہَے؟
ex.r: tumhāre mũh mẽ kyā hai?
ex.e: What's in your mouth?

ex.u: اُس نے میرے مُن٘ہ پَر پانی پھین٘کا۔
ex.r: us ne mere mũh par pānī phẽkā.
ex.e: He threw water on my face.

ex.u: سَماج کو کْیا مُن٘ہ دِکھائیں گے؟
ex.r: samāj ko kyā mũh dikhāẽ ge?
ex.e: We'll have no reputation in society!

ex.u: اُس کا مُن٘ہ بَنا ہوا ہَے
ex.r: us kā mũh banā huā hai
ex.e: He is in a bad mood.

منہ.e: First attested in c. 1503 as Middle Hindi مُن٘ہ (mnh, “indifferent”), inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀫𑀼𑀳 (muha), 𑀫𑀼𑀖 (mugha), from Sanskrit मुख (múkha, “mouth, face, visage”). Compare Punjabi مُون٘ہہ (mūṉh).

منہدم.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مُنهَدِم, from Arabic مُنْهَدِم (munhadim).

منیت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مُنْیَت (munyat), from Arabic مُنْيَة (munya).

ex.u: وِزارتِ مُواصَلات
ex.r: vizārat-i muvāṣalāt
ex.e: Ministry of Communications

مواقع.e: Borrowed from Arabic مَوَاقِعْ (mawāqiʕ), plural of مَوْقَع (mawqaʕ).

موت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian موت (mawt), from Arabic مَوْت (mawt).
Inherited from Prakrit 𑀫𑀼𑀢𑁆𑀢 (mutta), from Sanskrit मूत्र (mū́tra). Doublet of مُوتْر (mūtr). Cognates Cognate with Sindhi مُٽُ (muṭu), Marwari मूत (mūt), Punjabi ਮੂਤਰ (mūtar).

موتر.e: Learned borrowing from Sanskrit मूत्र (mūtra). Doublet of مُوت (mūt).

senses.g: a given name for a dog

موتی.e: Inherited from Middle Hindi موتی (mvty /⁠motī⁠/) (c. 1503), from Sauraseni Apabhramsa मोतिउ (motiu), from Prakrit 𑀫𑁄𑀢𑁆𑀢𑀺𑀅 (mŏttia), from Sanskrit मौक्तिक (mauktika).

ex.u: موتی پِرونا
ex.r: motī pironā
ex.e: to string pearls

موتی.e: Inherited from Middle Hindi موتی (mvty /⁠motī⁠/) (c. 1503), from Sauraseni Apabhramsa मोतिउ (motiu), from Prakrit 𑀫𑁄𑀢𑁆𑀢𑀺𑀅 (mŏttia), from Sanskrit मौक्तिक (mauktika).
